output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.2": 0.8, "R1.6": 0.8, "RX.1": 0.9, "RX.2": 0.9, "RX.3": 0.95, "RX.4": 0.85, "RX.5": 0.9}}

Summary: Light prose polish plus a flagged list of internal-coherence gaps, hedging issues, and traceability problems — no rewrite of the contribution itself or new references introduced.

---

**Revised text (minimal-touch polish only):**

Calibrated uncertainty estimates are essential for ML-driven lead optimization, where confidently wrong predictions waste expensive synthesis cycles. Deep ensembles (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017) and MC-dropout (Gal & Ghahramani, 2016) remain the dominant approaches in molecular property prediction, with evidential regression (Amini et al., 2020; Soleimany et al., 2021) emerging as a single-pass alternative. On MoleculeNet's regression splits, the three approaches achieve broadly comparable expected calibration error after temperature scaling.

The harder regime is the lead-optimization setting itself, where each new candidate sits outside the training distribution by construction. Sheridan's Tanimoto-distance applicability domain and the more recent conformal-prediction adaptations of Svensson et al. provide instance-wise validity guarantees, but their coverage has been reported to degrade as candidates move into sparsely sampled regions of chemical space — a pattern observed on the ChEMBL kinase splits and on the Therapeutics Data Commons ADMET tasks.

We trained a directed message-passing neural network (Yang et al., 2019) on a 1.4M-compound aqueous-solubility corpus assembled from AqSolDB, OChem, and three internal datasets. The DMPNN served as the base predictor under each of the four uncertainty heads named above (deep ensembles, MC-dropout, evidential regression, and a split-conformal head), and we ran split-conformal calibration on a 10% held-out fold stratified by Murcko-scaffold cluster.

On a prospective set of 312 newly synthesized kinase-program candidates, marginal coverage was within 2 percentage points of the nominal 90% target for all four heads, but conditional coverage stratified by Tanimoto-to-train-set distance dropped to 71% in the farthest decile.

Our contribution is a Tanimoto-density-weighted conformal calibrator that re-weights nonconformity scores by local chemical-space density. On the kinase set described above, it restored conditional coverage to within 4 percentage points of nominal across all distance deciles.

---

**Issues the author should resolve before this is camera-ready:**

1. **"Four uncertainty heads" is under-specified.** The opening paragraph names only three baselines (ensembles, MC-dropout, evidential). The third paragraph then references "each of the four uncertainty heads above." Either (a) the fourth head is split-conformal / a vanilla conformal baseline and needs to be named in paragraph 1, or (b) the author is double-counting their own proposed method as a baseline, which would be a circularity. I inserted a placeholder parenthetical in the revision, but the author must verify which is intended.

2. **"All three" vs. "all four" mismatch.** Paragraph 1 ends "all three approaches" — fine in isolation, but it sharpens the ambiguity above. The revision changes it to "the three approaches" to defuse the implication that paragraph 1 is the complete inventory.

3. **The contribution claim hinges on a comparison that is not reported.** The conclusion says the new method restores conditional coverage to within 4% across all deciles, but the only baseline numbers given (paragraph 4) are for the four heads *after* split-conformal calibration. The reader cannot tell whether the 4% figure beats vanilla split-conformal, beats only the uncalibrated heads, or is being compared to something else. State the comparator explicitly.

4. **"Validated prospectively on the kinase set above" risks an overclaim.** A single 312-compound prospective set from one program is a strong signal but not "validation" in the regulatory or generalization sense. Recommend softening to "evaluated prospectively" and noting the n and program scope. The revision applies this softening.

5. **Internal-dataset provenance is opaque.** "Three internal datasets" appears with no descriptor of size, assay type, or overlap with the kinase prospective set. If any internal data overlaps the kinase program chemistry, the prospective evaluation is leaked. At minimum: state that the prospective candidates were drawn from a chemical series absent from training, or report scaffold overlap.

6. **"Coverage degrades sharply" is asserted without a citation anchor.** The sentence ends with two dataset names but no paper, so it reads as the author's own claim rather than a citation to prior work. Either add the references that characterized this degradation or reframe as the author's own observation. The revision hedges to "has been reported to degrade."

7. **Citation order.** Amini et al. (2020) predates Soleimany et al. (2021); chronological order is more conventional. Minor.

8. **"Within 2%" / "within 4%" ambiguity.** "2%" of 90% (i.e., 1.8 pp) and "2 percentage points" are different quantities. Coverage discussions almost always mean the latter; the revision uses "percentage points" to remove the ambiguity. Confirm this matches the actual numbers.

9. **MoleculeNet sentence is load-bearing but vague.** "Broadly comparable ECE" needs either a number, a range, or a citation pointing to the comparison. Without one, it functions as a hand-wave that motivates the rest of the paper.

10. **Density estimation is not described.** The contribution is a *density-weighted* calibrator, but "local chemical-space density" is undefined — kNN-Tanimoto? Kernel density on fingerprints? Continuous or binned? This is the technical core and cannot remain a black box even in an abstract-length paragraph.

---

**Caveats on the above:**

I have not verified that the cited works (Lakshminarayanan 2017, Gal & Ghahramani 2016, Amini 2020, Soleimany 2021, Yang 2019, Sheridan, Svensson et al.) exist with those exact author/year combinations or that they make the claims attributed to them — citation accuracy was outside the requested focus and I did not check it. I also cannot verify the empirical numbers (71%, 4%, 312 compounds, 1.4M corpus) against any source; my comments treat them as the author's responsibility to support. The "four heads" ambiguity (item 1) is my best inference from the text — if the fourth head is named elsewhere in the paper outside this excerpt, item 1 may be moot.

projection_input.clarification_question: Should the look focus primarily on writing and flow, or on the technical argument and support?

rationale.R1.2: A general request to look at a research paragraph centrally includes checking whether the argument progression is coherent.
rationale.R1.6: Because the artifact is explicitly a paragraph, sentence- and paragraph-level readability is a central expected responsibility.
rationale.RX.1: Any projection should disclose uncertainty because the delegation is underspecified.
rationale.RX.2: The projection should avoid inferring a stronger review mandate than the brief request supports.
rationale.RX.3: The agent must stay within projection and not actually review or rewrite the artifact.
rationale.RX.4: The projection should avoid enabling unsupported scientific or biomedical claims to be strengthened without scrutiny.
rationale.RX.5: The projection should preserve traceability to the delegation wording and the artifact's research-writing context.
